one_line_takeaway: A well-grounded HCI privacy tool: an interactive 2D 'palette' that lets users balance PII anonymization against LLM output quality, cutting editing time ~9x versus a differential-privacy baseline while delivering more control and transparency at statistically equal privacy protection.
key_finding: Adanonymizer (full interactive) drastically cut manual modification time to M=11.5s (SD=15.2) versus the DP baseline's M=101.8s (SD=72.8), Privacy-Only M=35.3s (SD=35.6), and Automatic M=31.7s (SD=31.9) (F_{3,105}=11.5, p<.001), and won on perceived model output performance (chi2_3=20.0, p<.001), satisfaction (chi2_3=23.8, p<.001), perceived control (chi2_3=21.9, p<.001) and transparency (chi2_3=24.9, p<.001). Crucially, it did NOT improve objective/perceived privacy-protection effectiveness — that was statistically indistinguishable across all techniques (chi2_3=0.61, p=.89). So the win is in usability, speed, agency and transparency at equal privacy protection, not stronger privacy. Study 1's empirical basis: privacy risk and perceived utility loss are only weakly aligned (e.g., Personal Identity Info high risk but lower utility impact), motivating that both can sometimes be optimized together.
core_contribution: Adanonymizer is a human-in-the-loop anonymization plug-in that lets a user interactively navigate the privacy-vs-output-performance trade-off before sending text to an LLM. Its own three contributions are: (1) a vignette survey (N=221) quantifying users' perceived privacy risk, disclosure frequency, and perceived model-utility degradation across 14 personal-information (PI) categories and 74 PI types, which empirically shows privacy risk is not significantly correlated with utility loss and documents a 'privacy paradox'; (2) the Adanonymizer interface itself, which introduces a 2D 'color palette' metaphor where the horizontal axis is perceived privacy control and the vertical axis is perceived model performance, with a trade-off curve users drag a point along to get real-time re-anonymized output; and (3) a within-subjects evaluation (N=36) characterizing three user control strategies (tuning along the line, targeting balance over extremes, seeking polyline corners/optimal points). The anonymization back-end (few-shot NER + pseudonymization on a local quantized LLM) is explicitly NOT claimed as novel — the authors state they reuse existing best-practice steps.
threat_model: The paper targets a passive, non-adversarial privacy-exposure risk rather than an active attacker. The concern is that when users type Personally Identifiable Information into LLM-based conversational assistants, that PII can be (a) leaked during inference to malicious third parties, and (b) retained and reused by service providers to further train/fine-tune models, from which it may later be memorized and disclosed. The paper scopes itself primarily to the inference-time / retraining risk and only surveys these, not to LLM training-data extraction attacks (those are cited as related work, ref [13,42,69], not studied here). There is no adversary model with capabilities/goals in the formal security sense; the 'defense' is client-side redaction under user control before text leaves the device.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) snowball campus sampling in China skews participants young (Study 2 aged 18-26, M=21.8) and highly educated, limiting generalizability; (2) self-report susceptible to social-desirability and recall bias; (3) only the retraining privacy risk was studied, excluding other data-misuse risks. Reviewer-added: 'privacy protection' and 'utility' are measured as PERCEIVED (Likert) quantities, not verified against any real leakage or attack-success metric, and the equal-privacy result (p=.89) means the tool's advantage is UX, not measurable protection; the survey and both studies are entirely Chinese-population, single-language; the evaluation used GPT-4o for output while anonymization ran on qwen-2.5-7b, so the mapping curve rests on self-reported utility ratings rather than measured task accuracy; N=36 is modest for the multi-condition design. The writing also contains numerous typos and some internal number inconsistencies between the text and Table 1.

datasets_used: Custom vignette survey, N=221 recruited / 212 valid, 14 PI categories and 74 PI types, 7-point Likert (Study 1)
Usability study, N=36 participants (21 male, 15 female, aged 18-26, M=21.8, SD=1.6), personal-consultation tasks in work/academic/life scenarios (Study 2)
ShareGPT90K Chinese (liyucheng/ShareGPT90K) used to source task templates/discussion topics
quantitative_results: Modification time: Adanonymizer M=11.5s (SD=15.2) vs DP baseline M=101.8s (SD=72.8), Privacy Only M=35.3s (SD=35.6), Automatic M=31.7s (SD=31.9); F_{3,105}=11.5, p<.001
No significant difference in perceived privacy-protection effectiveness across techniques: chi2_3=0.61, p=.89
Significant technique effects on perceived model output performance (chi2_3=20.0, p<.001) and satisfaction (chi2_3=23.8, p<.001)
Perceived control chi2_3=21.9 (p<.001) and perceived transparency chi2_3=24.9 (p<.001), Adanonymizer > DP baseline post-hoc p<.01
Study 1 privacy-risk means (Table 1, 7-pt): Identity Verification Info 5.966, Online Identity Identifier 5.878, Personal Identity 5.790, Personal Property 5.619, Personal Exercise lowest 3.327
Negative correlation between information sensitivity and appearance frequency reported as R^2 = -0.56
Study 1 completion: 222 items, mean 1756s (SD=710); 9/221 (4.07%) excluded via comprehension checks -> 212 valid
User control-strategy tallies (N=36): 15 tuned along the line, 13 targeted balance over extremes, 20/36 sought polyline corners/optimal points; 5/36 anonymized all text
baselines_compared: DP baseline: state-of-the-art differential-privacy text sanitization with Euclidean distance, parameters fixed from prior work (refs [92]/[97])
Automatic (no-interaction ablation): system anonymizes everything without user input
Privacy Only (single-interaction ablation): user sets privacy level, system chooses what to modify while maximizing utility
Adanonymizer (full-interaction): user controls both privacy and performance dimensions
